reportTitle: Avent Pacifier BPA Public-Source Case Note
productName: Philips Avent Ultra Air SCF085/60 public-source case note
decisionNeed: A parent trusted a familiar baby brand, then found public reports about BPA detection claims. The parent needs a structured way to decide whether to verify, monitor, replace, or seek official guidance.
evidenceGrade: Mixed public-source evidence: official manufacturer response plus secondary summaries of a consumer-test claim.
claimBoundary: This demo does not independently claim the product is dangerous. It records public-source case notes and source conflict for parent decision support.
summary: Treat the product as a source-conflict case, not a one-line safe-or-dangerous verdict.
Confirm exact model, batch, country of purchase, purchase date, and current manufacturer guidance before acting.
If confidence remains low, select alternatives by transparent material claims, age fit, recall status, and caregiver acceptance rather than by brand trust alone.

sources.name: Philips Avent support page
sources.claim: Philips states all Avent pacifier ranges are BPA-free and says DEKRA follow-up tests found no detectable BPA, including the discussed sample.
sources.use: Highest-priority source for the manufacturer's position; not an independent regulator finding.

sources.name: Kormedi article
sources.claim: Article reports dTest found BPA in several pacifier products and reports Philips Avent Ultra Air at 2 ug/kg while noting Philips disputed the result.
sources.use: Useful case trigger and summary; verify against primary test data when available.

sources.name: DongA Science English-translated article
sources.claim: Article summarizes dTest-reported findings and notes manufacturer responses.
sources.use: Corroborating context only; machine-translated page warns it may contain inaccuracies.

sources.name: Momddok_TV
sources.claim: Used as a practical parent-operations persona anchor.
sources.use: Lens only, not factual evidence for the case.

sources.name: Ha Jung-hoon's BbiBboBbiBbo 119 Pediatrics
sources.claim: Used as a pediatric evidence-caution persona anchor.
sources.use: Lens only, not factual evidence for the case.

sources.name: O2 Brothers TV
sources.claim: Used as a family logistics and caregiver-communication persona anchor.
sources.use: Lens only, not factual evidence for the case.

personas.name: Practical parent operator
focus: What to check on the package and product
How to keep purchase, model, and batch evidence
How to choose a fallback without panic buying

personas.name: Pediatric evidence caution
focus: Avoid medical diagnosis or unsupported danger claims
Separate chemical-risk concern from actual exposure assessment
Escalate to clinician or regulator when symptoms, recall, or product defect is involved

personas.name: Family logistics explainer
focus: Explain the decision to all caregivers
Keep a single replacement and cleaning policy
Reduce repeated debate during nighttime care

meceSections.name: Product identity
notes: Match exact model number, age range, package language, country of purchase, batch or lot code, and purchase channel.
The public case mentions Avent Ultra Air and SCF085/60; parents should not generalize to unrelated models without evidence.
parentActions: Photograph packaging, product, batch code, and receipt.
Check the latest official support page for the exact model.

meceSections.name: Chemical-risk signal
notes: Secondary summaries report dTest detected BPA in several pacifiers.
Philips states follow-up DEKRA tests found no detectable BPA across its pacifier ranges.
parentActions: Do not convert a source conflict into a diagnosis or panic claim.
Use the conflict to decide whether replacement improves parental confidence.

meceSections.name: Regulatory and standards context
notes: Secondary summaries discuss differences between baby bottle BPA restrictions and pacifier rules.
The report should prefer official regulator or standards text when available.
parentActions: Record which jurisdiction applies to the purchased product.
If a recall appears, follow regulator or manufacturer instructions first.

meceSections.name: Alternatives
notes: A replacement should be selected by material disclosure, age fit, shape acceptance, recall status, and cleaning instructions.
Brand familiarity alone is not enough evidence.
parentActions: Prepare two candidate alternatives that meet the criteria.
Test acceptance gradually if the child strongly prefers the current shape.

unknowns: Primary dTest full report details were not available in this demo.
Batch-specific information for the user's actual product is unknown.
Current local regulator guidance for the purchase country must be checked before final action.
decisionCriteria: Replace immediately if an official recall, product breakage, choking hazard, or clinician/regulator instruction applies.
Replace for confidence if source conflict creates unacceptable caregiver anxiety and a suitable alternative is available.
Monitor and verify if there is no recall, the exact product differs from the public case, and caregivers can document official guidance.
Avoid claims such as 'dangerous product' unless a cited authority directly supports that wording.
nextActions: Record exact model, batch, country, purchase date, and seller.
Check manufacturer support and local regulator recall pages for the exact product.
Choose alternatives using material, age, recall, fit, and cleaning criteria.
Share one caregiver note summarizing the decision and uncertainty.
footerNote: ParentPick Guard is an evidence-organization aid for caregivers. It is not medical advice, legal advice, a product endorsement, or a recall authority.
